result: I keep returning to the last handful of jasmine rice I spilled on the counter two nights ago; how five grains stick under the kettle’s rubber foot and dance across the fake granite every time I nudge it for tea, scattering like white punctuation marks into kitchen sentences I never meant to write. Each grain is a bead on an imagined abacus: one counting missed calls, two tallying unread subtitles at 1 a.m., three remembering the summer the swallows learned the shape of my open window and swooped straight through the apartment like neighbors familiar with every lampshade and lie. Outside, the city installs inflatable pylons again for the marathon nobody asked for, orange buoys bobbing down avenues already drowning in their own reflection. Meanwhile, my voice memos fill with half phrases—”after the scaffolding comes down,” “before the siren keys itself into the aria,”—that arrive whole at 3:40 but dissolve by breakfast, leaving only rice percussion and the fan’s persistent heart murmur. I tell myself it’s all practice for something larger, some eventual novel carved from streetlights, but mostly it’s just this kettle inching forward one grain at a time, reheating yesterday’s water while I pretend tomorrow is already rehearsing me. Somewhere a clock resets without my permission. The rice keeps migrating, a tiny diaspora seeking higher ground.

Each grain is a bead on an imagined abacus: one counting missed calls, two tallying unread subtitles at 1 a.m., three remembering the summer the swallows learned the shape of my open window and swooped straight through the apartment like neighbors familiar with every lampshade and lie. Outside, the city installs inflatable pylons again for the marathon nobody asked for, orange buoys bobbing down avenues already drowning in their own reflection. Meanwhile, my voice memos fill with half phrases—”after the scaffolding comes down,” “before the siren keys itself into the aria,”—that arrive whole at 3:40 but dissolve by breakfast, leaving only rice percussion and the fan’s persistent heart murmur. I tell myself it’s all practice for something larger, some eventual novel carved from streetlights, but mostly it’s just this kettle inching forward one grain at a time, reheating yesterday’s water while I pretend tomorrow is already rehearsing me. Somewhere a clock resets without my permission. The rice keeps migrating, a tiny diaspora seeking higher ground.
